family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)

description:
Kribbel-Krabbel, the crazy tile laying game.

Description from the back of the game box:
Each Kribbel-Krabbel ant consists of 3 parts: head, belly and rear end. Each part has another colour. Sometimes the head is red, the belly yellow and the rear end green, sometimes the head is green, the belly red and the rear end yellow. But all ants have something in common: a stenciled out hole. either at the head, the belly or the rear end. The game is quickly understood: each ant must be laid on top of another ant, and in a way that through the hole the same colour is visible. For example: an ant has a red head, yellow belly and blue rear end. The hole is in the head section. You can put this ant on top of two kinds of other ants: either one with a red head heading in the same direction as the ant You are laying - or You change the direction of Your ant and lay it on top of an ant with a red rear section. 
Who is the first to lay all his ant cards?

Description from the publisher:

The space hulk Kaerux Erameas has landed on Sacaellum, and a host of Tyranids have caught the scent of blood.

Warhammer 40,000: Conquest – What Lurks Below is the fourth pack in the Planetfall cycle, and here you'll find cards that give new significance to planet symbols and invite you to fight for your faction's goals. This War Pack also offers a new Tyranids Genestealer warlord and his eight- card signature squad as well as fifty-one other cards to swell your armies. In What Lurks Below, every faction gains new ways to wage war and new reasons to fight.
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)

description: Each Player gets a bear as figure. The game is a version of  Snakes and Ladders. Additional there are fields on the board,when reaching them  you can roll a second time, have to go backwards or to pause.

Piat Gin is a card drafting game with Rummy-like scoring at the end. Players are dealt 6 cards. They keep one, placing face-down in front of them, and then pass two from their hand face-up to the player on their left. This is repeated, keeping one passing two, until they only have three cards left in their hand. During this last hand (you'll have three cards) you'll keep one and only pass one. Once finished players will keep both of these cards to finish their set of cards. All players reveal their hands and begin scoring. The highest scoring hand wins and receives bonus points. Play to a set score, usually 100 points.

Piat Gin is the next game in the series of "alien card games", Konog being the first. Games designed around the premise of a fictional alien race and how they might go about making a game that reflects their culture, biology, ideologies and other aspects of the species. These games are imported and introduced to those of us living in the Core Systems by the explorers and pioneers returning from the frontiers.

The Camenni are a long-lived species, known for extreme patience and sudden bursts of well-coordinated action. Piat Gin is a simple past time, different from the more complex time-consuming games that the elders might take part in (games similar to Chess or Go in which single turns can take years or decades to accomplish). This is not a game that possesses lavish details or ornate components. Piat Gin is carried by Camenni adventurers due to its small size or used to teach children patience and to practice the observation of subtle interactions of others.

The card faces and their features are intentionally kept simple, possessing no complex Camenni script, only differing amounts of circles, triangles, or rhombus (3 suits, numbered 1-6). This is so the game can be taught to most any species that might be encountered (please note that this deck is not resistant to mucus, salivation, persperation, or extremely high body temperatures like standard "outrider" decks). Camenni will drag a game of Piat Gin on for hours and even days when playing with each other. Of course, we don't have that luxury and play a round in a few minutes.

War game on a European map.
Each player has a bunch of military figures (tanks, Artillery, Ships, ...)
The map is separated in different countries with each a number of cities.
The whole map shows lines which cross each other, each point (possible position for a figure) has 6 neighbors.
At the beginning the players choose countries and place one of their figures on each of its cities.
If the player has not enough figures to place one on each city he has to choose another country.
Each type of figure has it's own "length of move", "shot range" and "Value of points".
On players turn he can move each of his figures it's "length of move" and shoot on one of the opponents figures. Roll a die (5 and 6 hit). The opponent is allowed to shoot also with all of his figures which are in range, also with the hit one.
After this all hit figures are removed.
Then the Opponent has his turn with move and shoot and the first player may shoot back.
After each double turn it is checked if a player has conquered a capital City. If this is the case all opponent figures are removed from this country.
After 8 double turns there is a value of points calculated from the number of his cities. With this points new stones can be bought dependent on the "Value of points" of the different types. These can be placed on own cities.
The same calculation is done at the end of the game. The winner is the player with the higher number.
The game ends after a predefined number of rounds or if one player has lost all his figures.

The players have to save the coloured mice from the cat.
The mice are placed randomly on the board. After time to memorize theirs positions the mice are covered by hats. At the border of the board there are coloured holes. The players have to  move the hats with the mice under it to the hole with the same color as the mouse has. If the color fits the player gets one point, if not the cat captures this mouse.
The game ends when there is at most one, captured mouse. The player with most points is the winner.

boardgame: Mega Man: The Board Game – Time Man & Oil Man
description: Adds Time Man and Oil Man to Megaman: The Board Game. Includes both figures, level boards for each, minion tokens for each robot master's level, and 2 additional Megaman figures and decks which expand the game to 8 possible players.

You are in control of a country’s secret service agency, with a small team of spies to put to work. Your task is to use Intelligence cards to influence your country into taking wise decisions. The trouble is, enemy spies have infiltrated your country and are influencing it into taking less wise decisions! Develop your culture to make it easier to spot enemy spies, improve science to get access to more elaborate cards, further your military to increase the number of spies at your disposal, or invest in economy to pay for bonus actions, known as Secret Directives.

Aim: There are four different types of Intelligence cards which are used to make progress on  4 corresponding development tracks. The winner of the game is the first player to reach the end of any one of these tracks. As players progress up the tracks, which each have 10 spaces, they also unlock various benefits:

Economy: Increases income of coins. Coins are spent on bonus actions which happen at the end of a round.
Military: Increases number of spies you have. More spies means more actions can be taken in each round.
Science: Makes more cards available. There are three levels of cards, at the beginning of the game, players only have access to the most basic cards.
Culture: Means capturing enemy spies is easier. At the end of a round, players who have developed culture can roll one to three dice to try to capture enemy spies.

In addition to trying to progress on development tracks, players need to build a deck of cards made up of Intelligence cards and Action cards. Cards can be added to their deck through research actions, which allow players to take cards from a shared supply. Most of the time, cards that are played return to that players deck by reshuffling their discard pile. However, playing Intelligence cards into another player’s country removes that card from their deck more permanently, and can also slow their opponents progress on the  development tracks.

Want a trick-taking cardgame that feels like an Internet flame war?  If so, check out Trollin'.

In Trollin', the object of the game is to try to take as many trick cards as possible, without taking the most cards of the poison suit.  If a player takes the most cards of the poison suit during a round/hand, they score no points for the round.

The game is playable with a reduced set of standard playing cards.  In the 2 player version (the game is playable for 1-6 players), players play with Ace, King, Queen, Jack, and 10 of all four suits.  The deck also uses 2 Jokers of different markings that are part of the Trump suit, which is Spades in the game, and are of greater value than the Ace, which is the highest in the trump (Cards go from high to low as Ace, King, Queen, Jack and 10).  Players are dealt 9 cards each and 4 cards are not used and are placed face down, and out of play, for the round.  As more players are added, more cards are added to the deck used.

The game also uses Hearts as the Poison suit.  During a round, a player who took the most Heart cards scores zero points, while their opponent scores 1 point for each trick they won.  In case of a tie for Hearts won in a trick, players score 1 point for each trick they won.

The game also features several optional rules, one of which is that a player can declare themselves Trollin' before the first trick is played.  If a player declares Trollin', they score as many points as the number of trick they won, and their opponent(s) score 0 for the round, IF they player managed to take all available Heart cards that could be won.  If the player declaring Trollin' fails to win all available Hearts, then they score zero points for a round and their opponent scores 1 point for each trick they won.

The game is played for a set number of rounds, or to a target score.  The name of the game comes from the inspiration of the game, that is Internet flame wars and people trolling in forums on the Internet.  The poison suit was discussed by the designer in Universal Mutators as a play mechanism.

Urban Explorers: Teruel is a card game for 2 to 5 players. The goal is to pick up more points than your fellow players while exploring the city.

Starting from the center card, players will explore the cultural and touristic Points of Interest step by step, and they try to arrive to each place before the other players in order to collect the points.

Each one turn, players will play one card. Then, the current player can move his pawn in any direction using the cards as stepping stones. If the card where he finally stopped contains any point piece, the player will keep all of them.

The town is full of corrupt politicians, snitches and stoolies. Something has to be done; it's time to take control of City Hall! Each player leads a crime syndicate of Capos, Muscle & Wiseguys in this fast paced game for 2-4 players. Laughter rises as bodies fall in the turf wars of Gangsterville.

Each player runs their own crime family with a set of cards. On your turn, you can recruit more gang member by drafting cards, exchange cards with other players, or start a turf war.  Winning turf wars adsvances your gang towards the ultimate goal, capturing City Hall.  The first player to reach city hall takes over Gangsterville and is the winner.

The player the collects 5 Light Bulb tokens or Reaches the end of the Rainbow first wins the game. You collect Idea (light bulb) tokens by landing on them, or by getting a card giving you one, or by by thinking of ways to alter the game.

This promotional pack includes 10 Negotiator Cards and 1 special Abductor card to be used with the solitaire board game Hostage Negotiator.

Decide before the game begins if you would like to use a Negotiator card. You may select one of your choice or shuffle and take one at random.
Each Negotiator card has a special ability that can only be used ONCE during the game.
In general, using a Negotiator card will make the game slightly easier, but some in Series 2 will actually make it more difficult.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
This time it's different. A member of your own police force has taken hostages. He claims he's being framed for the recent murder of the Chief and youre the only one who believes him. Some of the hostages are starting to sympathize with him, making your job more difficult. Everything is riding on your shoulders.

In this exciting new abductor pack you will have to deal with hostages who get Stockholm Syndrome, which means they identify with the hostage taker. Saving those that want to be saved is hard enough! Will you be up to this new challenge?
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
The abductor has hijacked a bus full of people and is making a run for the border. You'll be forced to employ tactics you've never used before and deal with the challenges of negotiating in different locations. Are you up to the challenge? Innocent lives are depending on you.

In this exciting new Abductor Pack you will have to deal with a new card type: Location Cards! The abductor's bus will need to be stopped and the all new Tactics Cards are just the things you'll need to set up a road block or creat a diversion. Its high speed negotiation just waiting for you!
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
FIRE TEAM VIETNAM  :  a game for team / squad / platoon level during the Vietnam War  ( Rules, counters (US, NVA  1965 ), and Geomorphic Boards )

- the maps ( geomorphic ) will contain the various types of grounds of Vietnam

- the  counters  represents teams ( 1 to 3 men ), leaders, fire team,...in the first module, and after modules : vehicles, armor, helicopters, ....of US ARMY and, NVA.

With expansions : the Counters for USMC, ANZAC, VIETCONG and others geomorphic Boards

Scales :
- 1 inch = 5 meters
- A turn = 1 minute :

--- Orders Phase : Hidden (  Company, Platoons, Squads Leader, Teams)

--- Actions Phase Teams ( Fire, Movements, .....simultaneous )

Available on battlesprojectmaps.e-monsite.com

-----> Les Règles sont disponibles en Français
Jeu de Tramway. Nouveau Jeu de Societe is about horsecars and was made in the early 1900's. It is typical roll and move board game. The first player who cross 63 stops, wins the game.

boardgame: Operation Gertrud: The German Invasion of Turkey
description:
Operation Gertrud: The German Invasion of Turkey is a speculative two-player wargame about a German invasion of Turkey during the Second World War. The historical course of events during World War II precluded any likelihood that the Germans would invade their erstwhile ally, it is conceivable that a different turn of events could have precipitated a German Blitzkrieg into Turkey. The Turks were concerned about this possibility and they endeavored to remain neutral during the war. Unlike the First World War, the Turks could not hope to hold Istanbul in the event of a war, and any incursion by one side (Axis or Allied) would certainly invite an incursion by the other. The Turks were most concerned about Soviets, but if Hitler had conceived a notion of invading Turkey, the Germans' most daunting challenge would be the Allied response, not necessarily the Turkish Army. The Turks had been underestimated during the previous world war, and so it’s anyone’s guess how well they could have faced up to the Wehrmacht and Luftwaffe.

Components: 22x34 inch map, 176 die-cut counters.

World at War #49, April 2016

boardgame: Zhukov's War: The Decisive Middle Phase of the Eastern Front, July 1942 – July 1943
description:
Published in World at War magazine #50, Oct-Nov 2016.
'Zhukov’s War: The Decisive Middle Phase of the Eastern Front, July 1942 - July 1943 covers the pivotal middle period of the titanic struggle between Hitler's Germany and Stalin's USSR, from July 1942 - July 1943. Strategic options abound for both players; victory goes to the one best able to carry out the chosen strategy, while maintaining a reserve to contain sudden enemy action or exploit an opportunity. The map covers the eastern front from Leningrad to the Sea of Azov at 20 miles per hex. Turns cover one month each for most of the year, two months during winter. Most units are Soviet armies and German divisions, plus a few Axis satellite corps. The turn sequence is built around the random drawing of chits to determine which player moves. Each chit drawn allows a player to both move and attack, but with a twist. One side allows the player to move first, then attack, the other side flips the sequence. There are two twists to the mechanism. First, chits come in several varieties, with differing penalties or bonuses depending on the chit and the side chosen. Second, during certain turns, one player or the other gets bonuses, allowing selection of the first chit to open the action, and providing a different mix of chits reflecting the overall situation at that point in the war. Victory may occur at one of three times during the game; the end of the first German offensive, the end of the Soviet winter counteroffensive, and finally the end of the game, after the final German summer offensive.

Game Scale:
Game Turn: 1 or 2 months
Hex: 20 miles / 33 kilometers
Units: Division to Army

Game Inventory:
Two 22 x 34" full color mapsheets
Two dual-side printed countersheets (352 5/8" counters)
One 24-page Zhukov’s War rulebook

Solitaire Playability: Medium
Complexity Level: Medium
Players: 2 or more
Playing Time: 4-20
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Pacific Battles is a wargame series of several campaigns in the Pacific Theater of Operations in World War II. The system is placed at the operational level, and deals with land and air operations. This is a two player game. One player controls the Japanese, the other the Allies. Pacific Battles Malaya is the fourth game in this series, and it covers the entire land-air campaign for the lower peninsula of Malaya, and the city of Singapore, December 1941 to February 1942. The other games in the series are Guadalcanal (#23), Nomonhan (#32), and Shanghai (#42).

Components: 22x34 inch map, 280 die-cut counters.

World at War #51; December 2016 - January 2017 issue.

Strike & Counterstrike (SCS) is purpose-designed solitaire wargame that covers the Soviet counteroffensive against German Army Group Center (AGC) from early December 1941 to the end of April 1942. The single player actively commands the Soviet forces, while the rules system directs him in the deployment of the opposing German force. The player wins with the Soviets by seizing control of key territorial objectives. Games may also end in draws or German victories. Strike & Counterstrike shares the same system with Rampage, Stalingrad Cauldron, and Panzers East, there are differences in their details of play. That’s due to the differences in the time and space scales in the individual games, as well as the fact I’m always further developing my views in regard to the best particulars for use in Solitaire systems. Play each game in the system according to the specific rules presented for it.

Components: 22x34 inch map, 228 die-cut counters.

World at War Magazine #53, April-May 2017.

A game of galactic struggle for 3-6 players (with variants for two player and solo play) by Thomas Staudt. The battle mechanism is taken from Button Men with kind permission by James Ernest.

Players represent the leaders of alien races battling for survival and supremacy in a system of six inhabitable planets. Eradicate your enemies, or simply suppress them with your superior numbers.
Each alien race has their specific powers and limitations that define their viable strategies.
Choose your fights and battle them out using lots of 6, 8, 10, 12 and 20 sided dice.

User summary:    
In the game of スシコン (Sushicon), you win the game if you get rid of all cards in your hand before anyone else does.

This game also has an educational aspect. If you play it, you will learn the importance of three steps to use the internet safely: "STOP. THINK. CONNECT."

Players try to achieve their goals considering "STOP. THINK. CONNECT.", using a  limited resource of cards and their own skill of bluffing.

The rulebook for the game is distributed for free by the designer at events promoting defense against cyber crime, such as the "Shirahama Cyber Crime Symposium", "Information Security Workshop in Echigo-Yuzawa". The rulebook has the URL to allow the player to download the cards used for the game.

Publisher's summary:
「SUSHICON」はいち早く手札をなくしたプレイヤーが勝利となるゲームです。使うのは、たった31枚のカード。
プレイヤーはインターネットを安心して利用するための習慣 『ストップ | シンク | CONNECT』を意識して、限られたカードとブラフ（はったり）を駆使して、ライバルを出し抜いて勝利を目指します。
このゲームをプレイすると、インターネットを安心して利用するための3つのステップ　「STOP. THINK. CONNECT.」　（立ち止まる | 考える | 楽しむ）の重要性について理解が深まります。

User summary:
Advanced Persistent Threat Challenge, or APT Challenge, is a card game for two players with a theme of Advanced Persistent Threat, a method of cyber attack.

Players assume the role of cyber attackers who are planning to steal confidential documents from each other. To achieve their goals, they have to read each other's hand, plan an effective set of targeted attack procedures, and conduct a cyber espionage.

After the game, add up the level of sensitivity of documents each player has stolen. The player with the higher total wins the game.

The rulebook for the game is distributed for free by the designer at events promoting defense against cyber crime, such as the Shirahama Cyber Crime Symposium. The rulebook has the URL to allow the player to download the cards used for the game.

Publisher's summary:
Advanced Persistent Threat Challengeは、サイバー空間におけるAPT(Advanced Persistent Threat)をテーマにした二人用の対戦型カードゲームです。
それぞれのプレイヤーは、攻撃者（スパイ）となり、機密文書の奪取を狙って、互いの手の内を読み、標的型サイバー攻撃の手順を組合せサイバー・エスピオナージ（諜報活動）をしかけ、勝利条件を目指します。
機密文書を奪取し、そこに定義された機密度の合計値を比較します。より大きな合計値のプレイヤーが勝利となります。

Fish'n'Stones: Heron is a promotional card for Fish'n'Stones that is available in Plato Magazine N°79 (september 2015) and at the Logis booth during Spiel 2015.

The heron, a long-legged bird, is shuffled into the deck instead of one empty water card, and it steals one random card out of an opponent's bucket of your choice at the end of the game.

A game requiring a sharp eye and a quick hand !

Where you need to be the first to find the card with all of the elements appearing on the dice. Be careful and be quick because although the cards may look alike, they're all different! Keep your eyes open, and beware of the traps !

Each round, the dice are rolled, and players search for a card that has all the elements that are on the rolled dice. The first player that finds the correct card may advance his score marker. When a player reaches the last space on the score track, he wins the game!

18 Somewhere

A kinder, gentler 18XX designed for to play well with 2 - 4 players and with new 18XX players in mind.

1. Rusting is replaced with maintenance.  
2. The inability to rust or scrap your last train combined with restrictions on train selling between companies makes company dumps not particularly painful.  
3. Companies with special powers gives a euro-y feel to it.

However, so that it's also interesting to experienced 18XXers,

1. There are 3 levels of play, basic, advanced, and full.  
2. The advanced level adds player controlled 2-, 5-, and 10-share companies twisting the availability of shares and the benefits of lots of early cash.  It also adds an auction for starting companies which evens out the initial priority.
3. The full game adds bonds (basically loans from players to companies).  Bonds do not count against the certificate limit giving early cash an added benefit and giving companies another way to raise money.
4. Although, in theory, train maintenance reduces the stress of the train rush, limiting the number of all trains (including the biggest brown trains) means the late train rush is brutal, often going from the first brown train to the last in less than one OR.

Replayability and scaling by player count is also covered:

1. Although the basic map is static, 4 of the 7 big dollar city locations vary from game to game.  In addition, players choose their starting city when they start their RR.  All this changes optimal routes from game to game.
2. The size of the map and the tile count scales by the number of players.  This also changes which big dollar cities and which RRs are included from game to game.

Play time:
The game is designed to be from 90 - 180 minutes although it needs repeated plays to achieve.

In this comic book-themed game, the world’s most nefarious and diabolical super-villain has been captured and imprisoned by meddling heroes. As fellow villains bad guys, it's up to you to orchestrate an escape!

Villains & Henchmen! is a (mostly) cooperative board/card game for 1-4 players, in which you acquire super powers, battle heroes, and overcome obstacles to break the super-villain out of a maximum-security prison.  The game includes four (4) different prisons, with their own set-up rules, victory conditions, and defeat conditions.

In order to accomplish their goals, players draw a hand of 5 cards from the communal "Villain" deck.  These cards include Attack cards, Defense cards, and Power Surge cards.  Attack cards are played on heroes, guards, and obstacles in order to defeat them.  Defense cards are played to overcome certain obstacles, as well as protect players' hands from the hero & guard abilities that otherwise make them discard cards and reduce players' effectiveness on their turns.  Power Surge cards are used to overcome certain obstacles, but more importantly are used to purchase super-powers.

Players can acquire different combinations of abilities, choosing from among 22 unique super powers.  These powers run the range of classic powers, including Super Strength, Time Control, Energy Blasts, and others.  Powers allow players to break or augment the normal rules of play, making their hand of cards go further and hit harder.

As the players defeat heroes and guards, they acquire victory points.  If the players succeed in defeating the scenario, the player with the most Villain Points gets bragging rights as the "winner", claiming the title of villain and relegating the other players to mere henchmen!  If the game wins, then all the players lose regardless of points, so teamwork is essential.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Exert your influence and guide the swirling mass of the smallest bits of matter back together to form sub-atomic particles while racing to build the biggest atom.

Quark is a lighter weight card game. Players have two actions each turn they can use to collect quarks, decay quarks down into other quarks, annihilate quarks for energy or to create particles.

At the end of the game, players score points for particles created, the best atom created and any energy they have left over.
Shakespeare: Prospero is a single promotional card for Shakespeare that doubles the effect of one costumed character.

boardgame: The Game + The Game on Fire
description:
Note: This entry is for compilations that contain both The Game and The Game on Fire

Players in The Game try to discard all 98 cards in the deck onto four discard piles in order to win, but they need to do so in the right ways.

Each player starts with 6-8 cards in hand depending on the number of players, and four discard pile prompt cards are on the table: two showing "1" and an up arrow and two showing "100" and a down arrow. On a turn, a player must discard at least two cards from hand onto one or more discard piles, with cards on the 1 piles being placed in ascending order and cards on the 100 piles being placed in descending order. One tricky aspect to play is that you can play a card exactly 10 higher/lower than the top card of a discard pile even when you would normally have to play in a descending/ascending order, e.g., if a 100 discard pile is topped with an 87, you can play any card lower than 87 or you can play the 97.

After a player finishes their turn, they refill their hand from the deck. During play, players cannot reveal exact numbers in their hands, but they can warn others not to play on certain discard piles or otherwise make play suggestions.

Once the deck is emptied, players are required only to play at least one card on a turn. If you play all 98 cards, you win! If you get good, the rules suggest that you play at least three cards a turn to increase the challenge.

The German NSV edition of The Game on Fire is a special version of The Game packaged in a tin with a playmat and an "On Fire" mini-expansion. This expansion consists of six flaming cards (22, 33, 44, 55, 66, 77) that replace the regular cards, and when one of these fire cards is played onto a pile, that card must be covered by the end of the next player's turn or else players have lost the game.

The U.S. edition of The Game on Fire will have the six flaming cards in the box in place of the other cards, and it won't contain the playmat.

The players start at the space of their color and have to move along the streets around the board, while minding the traffic rules. Movement is determined by a die. The first player to return to his starting point wins the game.

The players have to get from space 1 to space 120. Movement is determined by a die. On the circular spaces with pictures, events occur, that hinder or benefit the players. The first player to reach space 120 wins the game.

boardgame: Tea House of the Blue Moon
description:
A Tea House is a place where patrons come to drink, eat and be entertained by honorable, traditional Japanese female entertainers who act as hostesses; and whose skills include performing various Japanese arts such as classical music, dance, games and conversation, mainly to entertain male customers.

The game starts with a "Tea Table" in the center of the table. "Patrons" are randomly drawn, and placed at each of the six place settings. Each player starts with three cards; and each turn, players must play one card onto a patron, and then draw one card.

It is most important that the patrons are kept happy. A happy patron gives generous tips. Each patron favors or dislikes some of the services that the entertainers provide, so serve each patron in a manner to make them most happy.

You entertain the patrons by playing cards onto a patron of your choice, which adds the promise of a tip. The amount of the tip increases as additional entertainment cards are played on the patrons. A player can only collect the accumulated tips on a patron by playing a "Collect Tips" card.

Once a player has collected enough Yen to end the game (based on number of players), they may choose to end the game by saying: “I win.” All players finish the current round, and the player with the most Yen is the winner. If players are tied for the most Yen, they share the victory!

Winner of the August 2015 24-Hour Game Design Challenge! Revisions and tweaks being made to publish on The Game Crafter.

Desafío Energético is a family boardgame about the development of the oil industry and the distribution of its products to different regions in Argentina.

It's a game of two to four players with a duration of 30-45 minutes. 
The goal is to get as many points through the fuel supply to different provinces , building refineries and developing production areas.

To supply a province, the player must place chips in each area of the production chain : exploration, production , refining and transport networks , which connect these boxes with different provinces.
During his turn , the player can make one of two choices:

     Take two cards from the deck.
     Change 3 or 4 equal cards to place tiles on the board and expand the network.

Somewhere in our galaxy, a small, starless planet wanders aimlessly inside of a vast nebula.  Highly advanced satellites that the inhabitants think of as gods - “Lorestars” - orbit this world, periodically irradiating the surface in an endless cycle of long-abandoned experiments.  The native lifeforms and human settlers live a primitive state of violence because of the volatile effects that the satellites have had on their genetics over the centuries.  When specific satellites are in certain parts of their orbits, the bursts of radiation even cause some inhabitants to change into alien monsters.

In this part of the Lorestar story, you play as one of five unique characters that turn into savage creatures when their satellite god passes through the right constellation.  Vying for survival in the wild outskirts of small towns, some of these characters, like the planet they live on, are confused wanderers, while others take advantage of their abilites to ruthlessly hold power over weaker people.

Control the position of the Lorestar icons by outsmarting your opponents in a simultaneous card draw that spins the central game board.  Once your character has turned into a monster by aligning the Lorestars in the inner board to the constellations on the outer board, play attack cards and use your monster abilities to continue to foil your opponents and dwindle their health until you're the last monster standing.

Survive this battle and unlock the next chapter of Lorestar!

boardgame: Wings of Glory: World War 1 – Airco DH.2
description:
From the publisher:

The Airco DH.2 was a single-seat biplane “pusher” aircraft that was the first effectively armed British single-seat fighter. It enabled Royal Flying Corps (RFC) pilots to counter the “Fokker Scourge”, that had given to the Germans air superiority in late 1915. The DH.2 and similar aircraft carried the burden of fighting and escort duties. Great Aces gloriously piloted this aircraft: Robert Saundby, John Oliver Andrews and Lanoe Hawker.

Designed to be used with the popular air combat game system created by Andrea Angiolino and Pier Giorgio Paglia, each WW1 Wings of Glory Airplane Pack is a ready-to-play model, painted and assembled, 100% compatible with any other WW1 Wings of Glory game product. In each pack you will find all you need to play with the airplane: a special base with gaming stats, a variable altitude flying stand, and a specific deck of maneuver cards.

boardgame: Rise of the Kage: Docks of Ryu
description:
Rise of the Kage: Docks of Ryu expansion sees the ninja of the shadow wind clan come up against the brutal Minato and his trusted dock guards. Make your way through junks, warehouses and markets on your way to the summit of Ryu. With new maps, custom missions, guards and ninja the adventure continues in Rise of the Kage: Docks of Ryu.

This expansion includes:

20 plastic miniatures

1 double sided game board

16 game cards

4 player mats

Note: Rise of the Kage: Docks of Ryu is an expansion and requires the base game of Rise of the Kage.

Test your friends and family in football knowledge. Based on the classic "YOU ARE THE REF" strip from The Observer, you take on the role as a football referee.

As you move along the board you will pick up scenario cards based on the laws of the game.

Each scenario has three possible outcomes but only one of them is correct. The question is which? You have to be quick though, as in football, you don’t have long to think about your decision; only 15 seconds or the game moves on.

Each time you answer correctly, you will get a match card in the colour of the field.

The first to visit all six different colours and answer correctly will win the game. But be careful; you can be challenged in your knowledge by your opponents and might have to hand over one of your match cards.

All the scenarios and answers in the You are the Ref board game have been written by international football referee Keith Hackett and the scenario cards are accompanied by the art of Paul Trevillion.

Extra gift promotion for the Piñata party.
Additional moving mechanism card to the base game.

Description from the publisher:

Theseus: The Dark Orbit – Hunters, the second faction expansion for Theseus, contains a faction that employs trickery and subterfuge to their advantage. Hiding in the shadows, the Hunters can steal bonus cards and upgrade tokens, use enemy cards through hacking, and remotely trigger their own action cards. The Hunters also have the unique ability of leaving Theseus by hiding in their capsule just outside the space station.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
In EverDark, players make their way through the forest (represented by a single deck of cards) and try to assemble the highest valued hands possible by collecting special combinations of cards.

It won’t be without surprises though, as several negative-valued wolves are on the prowl, and a notorious thief calls the forest home.

If any bridge could be emblematic of the paratrooper drops of June 6, it would be Pegasus Bridge. With the 6-scenario campaign in this Scenario Pack, relive this formidable airborne operation, and command your Red Devils to take this crucially important objective.

Contents:
• Oxfordshire & Buckinghamshire Light Infantry (6th Airborne Div. 6th air landing brigade 2nd Battalion) and its recruitment options
• Somerset Light Infantry (6th Airborne Div. 5th parachute brigade 7th Parachute Battalion) and its recruitment options
• 22nd independent parachute company
• Field Command (Lt. Colonel Michael W. Roberts)
• 2 heroes (William Sutton and John Hollington Grayburn)
• New vehicles, equipment, character traits and orders for your paratroopers
• Sherman, Cromwell MK4, 6 Pounder AT gun and 75mm Pack Howitzer
• Flares and sentinel for the Germans
• Trenches
• The café Gondrée
• 6 terrain tiles, including Pegasus Bridge and Horsa Bridge
• Rules for night combats, drops, sleeping units, gliders…
• 6 scenario campaign
• Rules and scenarios are both in french and english
family: Series: Heroes System Tactical Scale World War 2

boardgame: Heroes of Normandie: Civilians Under Fire
description:
Description from the publisher:

During WWII, civilians from every type of background were displaced and caught in the line of fire.

Heroes of Normandie: Civilians Under Fire contains four punchboards of tokens that allow you to add the Mayor, the Priest, and more to your campaigns. Use the new recruitment tiles with terrifying Ilda, the She Wolf of the SS, or heroic Sandy leading the French resistance group, The FFI. Never underestimate the power of the civilians!
family: Series: Heroes System Tactical Scale World War 2

boardgame: ASL Comp: Afrikakorps – Battle of Gazala
description:
(from the introduction:)

Welcome to ASLComp AFRIKAKORPS, an ASLComp Variant series of scenario packs. All of the scenarios in this series are played on one or more copies of the 22" x 34" TOBRUK desert maps and using an all new set of overlays.

This expansion includes a map, counters and scenarios. Free bonus die-cut counters are provided in AK #1 and #7.

The collection of 100 historical scenarios depicts actions across the length and breadth of the Western Desert and Tunisia during World War II.

Strategic Decision: World War II covers the entire Second World War, from Poland to Nagasaki, from the Panama Canal to Bombay to Archangelsk to New York. High Strategic level gaming at the Army/Army Group level, and with unit production. No dice, no cards, nothing random used - you've only got yourself to blame if you lose! Two joined vinyl water-resistant maps: the Pacific (~21" x 14") and European/African/MidEast (~27" x 20") theatres are included.

For 2-6 players, the belligerents include Germany, Britain, France, Italy, the Soviet Union, China, Japan, the USA and major neutrals such as Poland, Yugoslavia, Greece, Turkey, Sweden and Spain. In the two-player game, it's the Axis (Germans, Italians, Japanese) against the Allies (USA, Britain, Soviets, France, China). There are three counter sheets of 88 5/8" counters each representing the land, air, naval and logistical units, mostly at the Army/Army Group level (infantry, tank/panzer, mech, shock, guards, SS, etc), with specialty units such as marines and paratroops. Air units represent tactical attack aircraft and strategic aircraft, including heavy strategic aircraft (B-29) and even atomics. The naval forces are flotillas of fighting ships, carrier task forces, landing craft and convoys. Logistical units play a major role in the game - no supplies, no attacks.

Turns are quarterly, and proceed over two maps, one of Europe/North Africa/Middle East and one of Asia and the Pacific, with the US East Coast on the former and the West Coast on the latter. Units appear historically, but their entry can be altered when the players change historical invasion dates. There are many political rules that reflect the limits that the historical leaders faced.

As units are eliminated, they enter replacement pools and it is then up to the players to decide what branch gets priority in production: will the German player seek panzers for the Eastern Front, supplies for North Africa, or Tactical aircraft vs Britain? The allies can share resources up to a point, and lend lease may be all that keeps the Soviets from folding early.

Players are free to try alternate strategies. Should the Germans hit Turkey to open a back door to the Middle East, the Suez and Baku? Should the US hit Japan hard early, or put the emphasis on stopping Germany? Should Japan join Germany's crusade against bolshevism or put the emphasis on naval victories?

Weather is incredibly important, affecting every turn except Summer. Winter slows everything down - except the Soviets.

There are no dice in this game! Hitler didn't roll the dice, and neither did Stalin, Roosevelt, Hirohito, Mussolini or Churchill. They made decisions and lived with them. You'll do the same. There are no randomization of effects at all. Each of your actions determines your fate, as does your opponent's. There is no dice to bail you out or ruin your plans - you have only yourself and your opponent to blame for that!

Yet each game takes a different path. It is possible for Italy to be the last Axis player standing. It's possible for the Allies to knock Japan out a year early or liberate Eastern Europe without the Soviets. The Soviets might just take all of Europe. All of these outcomes happened in actual games. Each turn requires you to plan ahead, to anticipate enemy moves and your counter to them.

You’ve got four basic shapes and only ONE MINUTE, can you doodle something different over each one? Rapidoodle is the fast-moving picture drawing game where drawing different wins!

Players all start with the same four shapes, then doodle over them to create four different pictures. If no one else doodled the same picture, you score! Rapidoodle challenges players to be original while fighting the clock. When creativity is king, will your doodles be one-of-a-kind? The more creative you are the more you win.

A press-your-luck dice game where you earn chips based upon the results of rolling four dice. Every time you roll two or more matching dice, you earn chips. You can choose to reroll as many times as you like to earn more chips but as soon as your roll shows no matches, you bust and lose the chips you earned that turn.

When you bust, roll a single die and note the number, then select one of the upright cups from the center of the table. Either keep the cup for yourself or give it to another player. Whoever takes the cup flips it upside down and either loses, gains, or steals chips equal to the number rolled. Play continues until all cups are flipped over (with a special rule for the last cup). The player with the most chips wins.

From the publisher:

One of the most popular traditional games. Made up of 26 bottle-caps, 2 halves of a ball and cardboard discs on to which the caps must be thrust. Drawing a football pitch on the floor, take it in turns to flick the bottle-caps into your opponent’s goal. Presented in a cloth bag and a vintage style cardboard box.

Word Shuffle is a spelling game, but one that can be played in English, French and Spanish. Words are scored based on letter value and the use of wild cards, and words from all three languages can be played simultaneously.

From the Publisher's site:

An original way to play this classic game of dominos. When the pieces are placed together they form a circle. The vintage illustrated cardboard pieces create a different scene with each game. Presented in a vintage style cardboard box.

Get into this crazy race where you try to save eggs from the frying pan and take them back to the safety of their nest. Two players, at the same time, will try to move the eggs without breaking them. Use the Chicken Magnets to carefully lift the eggs and take them back to the nest. The player with the most unbroken eggs will be the winner!

Players each clip the magnet, dangling by a string, to their belt/waistband, like a tail. They squat and pick up an egg from the pile in the frying pan. Then, carefully, they walk/waddle over to their "nest", and gently, without using their hands, use the nest to separate the egg from the magnet. If the egg drops, it will break apart, and the yolk will spill out. Players compete to rescue the most eggs.

From the rulebook:

The object of this game is for the players, using dice and playing pieces, to travel on the gameboard from a residence square to the bank and shopping locations shown on their shopping lists. The first player to return to his residence with the travel cards for all locations contained on his shopping list wins the game!

boardgame: Wings of Glory: World War 1 – Fokker E.V
description:
From the publisher:

The Fokker E.V was a German monoplane fighter aircraft designed by Reinhold Platz and built by Fokker-Flugzeugwerke.

The E.V was the last Fokker design to become operational with the Luftstreitkräfte, entering service in the last months of World War I. After several fatal accidents due to wing failures, the aircraft was modified and redesignated Fokker D.VIII, and it had the distinction of scoring the last aerial victory of the war. It was flown by famous aces such as Erich Lowenhardt, Theodor Osterkamp and Karl Sharon.

Designed to be used with the popular air combat game system created by Andrea Angiolino and Pier Giorgio Paglia, each WW1 Wings of Glory® Airplane Pack is a ready-to-play model, painted and assembled, 100% compatible with any other WW1 Wings of Glory game product. In each pack you will find all you need to play with the airplane: a special base with gaming stats, a variable altitude flying stand, and a specific deck of maneuver cards.

Hammer and Forge is an expansion for Advanced Song of Blades and Heroes or for Fightin' Fungi (the two rule sets are identical except that Fightin' Fungi has additional rules specific to fungus creatures).

Hammer and Forge provides additional rules, background and profiles for the Dwarf race and its allies and enemies.

The Hammer and Forge supplement was originally released through a Kickstarter campaign which also funded the production of a line of miniatures (primarily Dwarves), but as is standard in the Song of Blades and Heroes system, players can use whatever miniatures they prefer or have on hand.
family: Series: Song of Blades and Heroes

description: A crossword game in which up to four players build word grids on a  grid of coloured squares bearing preprinted letters. Each word must match a pre-determined theme and points are scored for word length with bonuses for matching letters on the board and for starting a word on the player's colour.

boardgame: Bolt Action: Empires In Flames – The Pacific and the Far East
description: Far from the battlefields of Europe and North Africa, Allied forces fought a very different war against another foe, from the jungles of Burma to the islands of the Pacific and the shores of Australia. This new Theatre Book for Bolt Action allows players to command the spearhead of the lightning Japanese conquests in the East or to fight tooth and nail as Chindits, US Marines and other Allied troops to halt the advance and drive them back. Scenarios, special rules and new units give players everything they need to recreate the ferocious battles and campaigns of the Far East, from Guadalcanal to Okinawa, Singapore, the Philippines, Iwo Jima and beyond.

boardgame: Bolt Action: Germany Strikes! – Early War in Europe
description:
In 1939, Germany shattered the peace of Europe with a lightning-fast strike against Poland. The next year, it captured Denmark and Norway, before launching its famous Blitzkrieg against France, Belgium and The Netherlands. In less than two years of fighting, Nazi Germany became the master of mainland Europe.

This new Theatre Book for Bolt Action allows players to command armies of German tanks driving across the continent or to lead the desperate defense of the outgunned Allied armies. New scenarios, special rules and units give players everything they need to recreate the devastating battles and campaigns of the early war in Europe, including the fall of Poland, the breaking of the Maginot Line and the dramatic retreat to Dunkirk.

boardgame: Bolt Action: Duel In The Sun – The African And Italian Campaigns
description:
With Duel in the Sun, players can take command of the doughty Desert Rats of Montgomery's Eighth Army, the fast-moving and hard-hitting raiders of the Long Range Desert Group (LRDG), or Rommel's mighty Afrika Korps to recreate some of the most iconic battles of World War II--Operations Compass, Crusader and Torch, Tobruk, and Alamein among others.

Offering scenarios, special rules, and new troop types, this theater book for Bolt Action also takes players across the Mediterranean from North Africa, where they can follow the Italian Campaign from the invasion of Sicily, through the battles for Anzio and Cassino, to the final assaults on the Gothic Line.

boardgame: Mara und der Feuerbringer: Das Kartenspiel
description:
"The world threatens to sink into the dark and everything still between her and Ragnarok, the Twilight of the Gods, is, you are. Uses as your visionary skills, transgress with the gods in contact and find them, to bring them back to their homeland. Yet all is not lost! But be careful, the gods do not act entirely altruistic. They will try to influence you with deceit and cunning to get at your rune stones and fight hostile gods. Help your clan to strengthen their claim to the throne of Asgard and place the world order restored-because everything depends on you."

With Mara und der Feuerbringer: Das Kartenspiel ("Mara and the Fire Bringer: The Card Game") you embark into the mystical world Maras and the Nordic Germanic mythology. 2-4 players are stalwarts and assume the role of a Spákona and try to stop Ragnarok and to help their respective clan to the throne of Asgard.

Each player tries to collect the most victory point with his cards on the table, victory points stack and rune stones.

Victory points can be gained by playing your own authority cards, but also by stealing opponent cards and rune stones.

The game ends as soon as the first player has 14 victory points.

Players begin with playing their first mythological place open. Afterwards, each player in turn takes on one of the four stacks in the middle of the table, chooses three more cards in hand (without changing the order of cards in the stack) and then puts back these. Now, each player has five cards in his hand and a mythological place in front of him. It is not allowed to have more than 5 cards in hand. This hand limit, however, can be increased by certain card abilities.

The game is played as round clockwise. Each turn consists of four phases:

1. Energy Phase
2. Expansion phase
3. Action & Combat Phase
4. Draw cards
A compilation of Munchkin Cthulhu, Munchkin Cthulhu 2 and Munchkin Cthulhu 3, along with 4 unique Dutch cards.

The BlutKreuz Korps has arrived in the Middle East! Along with the rest of Operation Babylon's forces, the dreaded Korps is now fighting the Allies and the SSU in this part of the World.

This first expansion to Treasure Hunter adds new Treasure Tiles, interesting new Hero Cards, tougher goblins, and Dog Cards to chase those pesky goblins off!

Yokai: Yamata No Orochi is the first expansion for the card game Yokai. This expansion add more white cards and the new black Yokai, a new type of cards that give negative points to the players.
The expansion add also a new game modality, the "Orochi Challenge", in this modality all the special powers of the cards and the points value are nullified, the goal of the players is to not make a tricolor trio and make or a monocolor trio for empty the board.

When a player make a tricolor trio, he must draw one of the nine cards that are on the board, in a case of a stall all the players draw a card, if the card is black the player was eliminated.

Players roll six-sided dice numbered 2-6 with a cow symbol replacing the one.  Summing dice and doubling the score for each cow.  Only even-numbers count.  On each turn the player may roll up to 3 times, setting aside dice on each roll.

The Nebula From Hell is a party game for people who enjoy laughing at and being laughed at by others.  It is meant to be played with crazy, sarcastic people who enjoy making fun of other people. This game has it all. Nebulas, dragons, canoes, bombs, wizards, stars, action cards, bonus cards, physical challenges, and boats. What more could you want from a game?

This game actually hates you.  Most games try to entertain you.  This game wants to crush your skull. Just when you think you're winning, you'll be sucked into the Nebula and spit out with nothing.  Game over. Go home, loser.

Who is this game for? For those who prey on the weak and laugh and jeer as they kick them while they're down. If you have any emotional response at all in life, this game probably isn't for you.

Easy to play. Hard to win. On your turn, drawn an action card.  Read it out loud. Do what it says. That's it.

From the publisher:

Bota Abaixo is your new card game!

Four friends, inebriated by another tedious evening decided to look for the cure for boredom creating this amazing game.

With this game you will finally find a way out of those awkward silences on stuck-up events. Bota Abaixo is the solution to turn the most monotonous of parties into a cascade of good vibe!

Bota Abaixo is the game that can’t miss to liven up your parties!

Based on the most popular drinking games as Ring of Fire or Kings, Bota Abaixo is more than a game, it’s an appealing and singular object which allows you to play,  have fun and collect.
 PLAY TO THE LAST DROP !!

HOW TO PLAY:

The players get around the cards in circle and the cards are randomly put in the middle face down. Playing clockwise each round a player picks one card which will have one specific rule wich the player make something (usually drinking a swig). At the end of each round the cards are discarded with the exception for the WC and immunity cards which can be kept. The game is played until there are no more cards.

You can play in the Competition mode in which each player can only refill his glass one time and are eliminated as soon as they drink  it. The game is when until there is only one winner "standing".

There are various cards as Cascade; Make up one Rule; They Drink; Rime vs Rime...

Note: To play this game the use of alcoholic drinks is NOT mandatory.

Description in Portuguese:

Quatro amigos com jeito para a ilustração, inebriados por mais um serão enfadonho resolveram procurar a cura para o aborrecimento. E foi assim que de lápis na mão e de copo na beiça criaram o Bota Abaixo!

Baseado nos mais populares drinking games como Ring of Fire ou Kings, e ilustrado duma ponta à outra em 56 divertidas cartas, Bota Abaixo é o jogo que não pode faltar para animar as tuas festas!

Com Bota Abaixo vais conseguir quebrar o gelo naqueles convívios chatos onde apenas se trocam olhares macambúzios. É a tua solução para transformar a mais monótona das festas numa cascata de boa disposição!

JOGA  ATÉ À ÚLTIMA GOTA!!

This game was adapted from the british TV-series "Man in a Suitcase" (Der Mann mit dem Koffer) from 1967.

The players have to reach the safe in the center of the game board with their agent. Movement is determined by a die. If a Agent gets on an orange space, he advances according to the printed arrow. On a red or yellow space the player has to draw a card to hinder or benefit his agent. if he rolls a six he doesn't move, but has to drop the glass marble into the slot. Any agent that gets hit by the marble has to return to the starting space. The player who drops the marble has the option to remove his agent from the board and return it after the marble has run it's course. However if he chooses to remain on the board and his agent isn't hit, he advances a number of spaces, according to the compartment, where the marble comes to rest. The first player to reach the safe wins the game.

boardgame: The Princess Bride: Prepare to Die! Again!!
description:
A party game in which players fill in the famous phrase "Hello. My name is Inigo Montoya. You killed my father. Prepare to die."' with ridiculous names and activities.

You might say to yourself, "but wait, this game already exists!" and you would nearly be right ... but actually wrong.

Prepare to Die! Again!! is the exact same game as Prepare to Die, but with different cards.

A cross game promotional character from Artistic Justice Game's Dragon Tides, Bruce Lee wields four 'follow-up attack cards' which he can ante on any given beat and represent a second attack he will make if his first attack connects. While a fairly straightforward character who can be shut down with careful play, Bruce Lee's barrage of attacks gives him explosive damage potential on every single beat if his combos manage to connect properly.

Level 99 Games' official mascot character, Eliza has four fanciful genre cards which she can use when her attacks connect to add various effects to her attack pair. However, Eliza must be careful -- if she gets hit, her opponent can force her to discard a genre card, rendering it temporarily unusable. Eliza is a very tactical trickster style character, with a surprising amount of versatility and control over the battlefield.

BattleCON: Devastation's hidden secret bonus character, Anath Adrasteia represents the forces of Havoc come to wreak devastation across the land of Indines, in true easter-egg-secret-final-boss style. Anath wields four different stance-like aspect cards, one of which will always be active, which give her enormous passive stat bonus (and some penalties), which combine with her powerful but somewhat inflexible styles to turn her into stat monster worthy of being called a final boss.

BattleCON: War's hidden secret bonus character, Evil Hikaru is the twisted and dark reflection of the hero Hikaru. With four evil elemental antes mirroring Hikaru's pure elemental tokens but with more insidious, offensive effects, combined with his incredibly powerful styles and unique base, Evil Hikaru makes for an excellent hidden easter egg secret bonus boss.

A cross game promotional character from Arcane Wonders' Mage Wars, Iskra Brimstone is a warlock who takes delight in setting her foes on fire. When Iskra successfully stuns her opponent she also sets them on fire for three turns, causing them to take damage over time and also supercharging many of her already aggressive styles.

A cross game promotional character from Arcane Wonders' Mage Wars, Raederick Blackforge is a heavyweight fighter with four unique equipment cards he can forge for himself as the match goes on. These pieces of equipment, once made, grant Raederick permanent bonuses for the rest of the match, and can be sacrificed to give him huge temporary bonuses. With the help of his arms and armor, Raederick can transform himself from a run of the mill heavyweight into a veritable juggernaut!

A cross game promotional character from Plaid Hat Games' Summoner Wars, Prince Elien is a tactical fighter who does battle by summoning and moving around a wall marker which both protects him from his opponent's attacks and also allows him to launch certain attacks of his own from its position. With a number of ways to place a literal wall of protection between him and his enemy and to safely attack them from behind cover, Prince Elien is a defensive force to be reckoned with.

A cross game promotional character from Jasco Games' Red Horizon, Vincent Grey gets the opportunity to guess his opponent's attack pair, getting a small but sizable bonus if he gets it half right and outright stunning his opponent if he is completely on the mark. This, along with his hard hitting and surprisingly versatile styles and unique base, makes Vincent both a heavyweight fighter and a predictor fighter -- a combination which should strike fear into the hearts of BattleCON players everywhere.

A cross game promotional character from Jasco Games' Red Horizon, Nehtali is a ranged brawler with five unique dark deal cards with very sizable stat bonuses on them. During the ante phase, Nehtali may ante 2, 3, or all 5 of these dark deals by paying 0, 1, or 2 life, respectively, and then split the deals into two different stacks. Her opponent then chooses which stack to give Nehtali, giving her the bonuses of the deals in that stack. This unique mechanic combined with her high movement medium ranged styles turn Nehtali into a highly versatile and unpredictable brawler, capable of trapping her opponents into many different lose-lose situations they cannot easily escape from.

A crossover promotional character created by Nokomento (Eunice Tiu) herself, Lucius is a build up brawler fighter with three shackle cards which all begin the game locked. Whenever Lucius stuns his opponent with an attack, he may choose to deal no damage to instead unlock one of his shackles, granting him a permanent stat boost for the rest of the game. This, combined with his trickery, slippery styles allow him to slowly build up power until he can crush his opponents with the combined onslaught of unpredictable attacks alongside sheer numerical superiority.

A crossover promotional character created by Fabio Fontes, Takeshi Kamikaze has a stash of eight different weapon cards which he cycles through as the match progresses, each giving him radically different stat bonuses and penalties. With his various weapons giving him wildly different stats, Takeshi performs drastically different when wielding a baseball bat compared to a minigun compared to a butterfly knife, and this combined with the ease at which he permanently discards one weapon to pick up another makes a fight with Takeshi a dramatic and unpredictable affair to participate in. Pick the right weapon for the immediate situation and use it as a silver bullet -- or, pick the one that gives your opponent the most fits, and use it as a can opener to pry them open.

Despotic is a fantasy themed 2 player spatial-tactical strategy board and card game. In Despotic, players assume the role of gladiator-like parties sent to battle one another for the Emperor's pleasure.

Unlike the abstract game of Chess, Despotic plays fast, adds some luck, probability management, decision limitation, and equalization(handicapping of Chess-Masters). In Despotic, the goal is to destroy your opponent's "King" or to run your opponent out of their 20 Power cards.  Losing a Unit requires the removal of Power Cards equal to the Unit's Value.  Also, Players remaining in Check must remove 2 Power Cards from their deck.

Initial set-up is like standard Chess, however each Player has 2 decks of cards (20 Movement Cards and 20 Power Cards).  Each Player starts with 3 Movement Cards.  During a turn, the player can (a) Play 1 card from their hand and resolve its effect, (b) Discard any number of cards from their hand and draw back up to 3 cards (Power and/or Movement Cards), -OR- (c) May remove any number of Dead Cards in their hand from the game, and then may draw and play 1 Power Card for each Dead Card removed.  At the end of a turn, the Player draws back up to their hand limit size of 3 cards (from either Power and/or Movement Cards).

Movement Cards allow a corresponding Unit to move as indicated (standard Chess patterns).  Power Cards allow non-traditional movement and/or create special effects/abilities in the game.

Chevalier: Medieval Battles is a wargame for two players which simulates the battles of Europe’s Middle Ages. Players take on the roll of a Medieval general, building their army, maneuvering them on the battlefield, and issuing special orders. The game uses unit cards to represent units in the players’ armies which are arranged on a unique play mat to represent the flanks, front line, and reserves of the army.

Players construct their armies by selecting up to eight unit cards representing fifteen different types of Medieval units. Each unit has a listed maximum army limit, type, equipment, and combat stats for their differing “status levels.” Players also select a hand of eight “orders cards” which can be issued to units during gameplay to move them to a new location on the player’s play mat or gain one-time buffs.

After players alternate deploying their armies on their play mats, combat occurs in a series of turns divided into an Orders phase (when orders cards are played and resolved) and Combat phase (when combat is resolved).

During the Orders phase, players simultaneously play any orders cards they wish to use face-down on the unit receiving the orders. Progressing in a set pattern determined by the play mat, these orders are then sequentially revealed and resolved.

Combat between units on the board is resolved following the same sequential pattern as resolving orders cards. To resolve combat, players add up the relevant combat skill for the current unit and any situational modifiers (such as the type of unit opposing a unit, whether the unit is charging that turn, or any orders cards affecting the unit) to determine how many dice to roll. Each die rolled which meets or exceeds the target unit’s current “defense” value for its current status level scores one “hit” and permanently reduces that unit’s status by one level. As each unit’s equipment listed on its card gives certain bonuses against certain unit types, often while imparting a penalty against other unit types, players can gain an advantage by careful use of their deployment and orders to pit their units against their optimal opponents. Units decrease in effectiveness as they take hits, becoming weaker in both offense and defense. Each unit has three status levels ranging from undamaged to heavily damaged. Any unit reduced by hits beyond its third status level is removed from the game.

The winning player is the one with the last unit remaining on the board.

Sifaka: The Tunnel is a promotional card pack for Sifaka.

To set up, shuffle one tunnel card into the deck and set the other one aside. This first tunnel is played as a normal card, after which the second one can be placed wherever the player wants. From this moment on, the tunnel can be used by every player. When you enter the tunnel, your sifaka comes out the other side and can be turned into any direction before you continue its movement.

Meet Linkee's little brother, Dinkee!

Kids love a quiz. It's just that most are too hard. Or too boring. But not Dinkee. It's easy to learn, loads of fun and everyone plays at once.

Simply answer four questions and work out what links the answers. Then shout DINKEE! Get it right and win the card. It is easy-peasy lemon squeezy.
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description:
In Thak! You are a dwarf miner with the goal of digging. Landslides, Cave-ins and Explosions could happen at any time. Others players will want to stop you, bring the floors down beneath you, but you need to dig.Dig to become King of the Mine!

Thak! is a card game composed by 126 cards for 2 to 6 players, which can be played all vs all, in couples or in teams. A game lasts about 15-30 minutes.

During the game players will Dig (draw and show) cards from the Deck (The Mine). While players are digging, everything and anything can happen: from finding a precious Rune or Tool to suffering a bad Landslide. The strategy and abilities of players will let them to become rich, to defend theirselves from dangerous things and hinders others players and the best of them will become King of the Mine.
